summary: This notebook demonstrates a distributed training pipeline for a seismic waveform inversion task, leveraging two GPUs via PyTorch DDP to maximize Kaggle's weekly quota and enable larger batch sizes. It implements a custom U-Net architecture with an HGNet-V2 backbone, incorporating temporal flip augmentation, Exponential Moving Average (EMA) for training stability, and Monai upsampling blocks. The author provides a complete workflow from data loading and preprocessing to validation and test-time augmentation (TTA) inference, along with three pretrained checkpoints. Readers should learn how to structure multi-GPU training in PyTorch, adapt backbone strides for U-Net compatibility, and effectively ensemble multiple models for robust predictions.
competition_flow: Loads preprocessed seismic waveform data, trains a custom U-Net with an HGNet-V2 backbone using 2-GPU DDP and EMA, validates on a fixed fold, and generates a submission by averaging predictions from three pretrained models with test-time augmentation.
data_reading: Loads preprocessed .npy files via np.load with mmap_mode='r' for training and standard loading for validation; metadata and fold assignments are read from folds.csv.
Test data is loaded from a directory of .npy files using glob.
data_processing: Resizes inputs to (70,70) using area interpolation and pads with replicate mode to reach (72,72), then converts to float16.
Applies a temporal flip augmentation during training that reverses both source and receiver dimensions for inputs and labels.
Uses flip-based TTA during inference by averaging original and flipped predictions.
models: Custom U-Net with HGNet-V2 backbone (hgnetv2_b2 for training, hgnetv2_b4 for checkpoints)
cv_strategy: Fixed holdout split using fold 0 for validation and all other folds for training.
ensembling: Averages the raw outputs of three pretrained models using a custom ensemble class during validation and test inference.
insights: Distributing training across two GPUs with DDP enables larger batch sizes and faster convergence while maximizing Kaggle's GPU quota.
Removing BatchNorm2d layers and adding intermediate convolutions in U-Net skip connections accelerates convergence and improves prediction quality.
Temporal flip augmentation must reverse both spatial dimensions of inputs and labels to preserve physical consistency in waveform data.
EMA provides more stable validation metrics compared to raw epoch weights.
